caption: Figure 1: The Transformer model architecture. Stacked self-attention and point-wise, fully connected layers for both the encoder (left) and decoder (right). The encoder is a stack of N=6 identical layers, each with a multi-head self-attention sub-layer followed by a position-wise feed-forward sub-layer, with residual connections around each sub-layer. (Vaswani et al., 2017 — Attention Is All You Need)

Figure 4: Annotation interface for human evaluation of factuality. A pop-out for detailed instructions and a worked example appear when clicking "view tool guide". (Lewis et al. 2020 — Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks)

Figure 4 (a): Root communities at level 0 — Graph communities detected using the Leiden algorithm (Traag et al., 2019) over the MultiHop-RAG (Tang and Yang, 2024) dataset as indexed. Circles represent entity nodes with size proportional to their degree. Node layout via OpenORD (Martin et al., 2011) and Force Atlas 2 (Jacomy et al., 2014). Level 0 corresponds to the hierarchical partition with maximum modularity. (GraphRAG)

Figure 4 (b): Sub-communities at level 1 — same Leiden-detected community graph as panel (a) but at the next level of hierarchical clustering, revealing internal structure within the level-0 root communities. (GraphRAG)

Figure 3: A retrieval and generation example for the LightRAG dual-level retrieval pipeline. When presented with the query "What metrics are most informative for evaluating movie recommendation systems?", the LLM first extracts both low-level and high-level keywords; these keywords guide the dual-level retrieval process on the constructed knowledge graph. (LightRAG)

Figure 4: Prompts for Graph Generation. The graph construction prompt extracts entity-relationship information from a text document by (1) identifying entities and categorizing them (organization, person, location, event), (2) describing their attributes and activities, and (3) identifying relationships between entities with explanations, strength scores, and high-level keyword summaries. (LightRAG)

Figure 2: TextRAG (left) vs. VisRAG (right). Traditional text-based RAG (TextRAG) relies on parsed texts for retrieval and generation, losing visual information in multi-modal documents. Vision-based RAG (VisRAG) employs a VLM-based retriever and generator to directly process the document page’s image, thereby preserving all information in the original page. (VisRAG)

Figure showing the portion of queries that have the positive document retrieved at the top-1 position ("Correct Retrieval"), and that are answered correctly given the top-1 retrieved document ("Correct Generation"). MiniCPM-V 2.0 achieves 30% higher performance than MiniCPM (OCR) when using ground-truth documents, underscoring the importance of visual clues for extracting answers from documents. (VisRAG)
